title: New devices for better managing cardiac rhythm

Shortness of breath is a symptom of heart conditions. Not everyone responds to cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT). Bioengineers at MicroPort CardioFlow have developed an advanced sensor-based device to improve CRT.Credit: Suphaporn/iStock/GettyAs people age, many develop cardiovascular diseases such as hypertension and arrhythmias. These can eventually lead to heart failure, which is a major contributor to cardiovascular deaths. More than 64 million people globally are living with the condition, according to recent estimates1.“Heart failure poses a serious social challenge,” says Yusuke Kondo, a cardiologist specializing in arrhythmia treatment at Chiba University Hospital near Tokyo. “With populations around the world ageing, the number of affected individuals is expected to grow — a trend often dubbed the heart-failure pandemic.”Pharmaceutical treatments may not be enough to treat all forms of heart failure. Interventions involving devices are often also required to restore coordinated heart contractions and alleviate symptoms including fatigue and shortness of breath. One such intervention is cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT), which uses special pacemakers to restore coordinated beating.Despite becoming an established standard of care in the early 2000s, not everyone responds to CRT. “About 30% of patients fail to derive significant benefit,” says Kondo2.“This lack of response to CRT represents a significant unmet need,” says Amel Amblard, vice president of Clinical Affairs at MicroPort CardioFlow, with its Cardiac Rhythm Technology Research & Development headquartered in France, that also has offices in Japan. “We’ve been working closely with clinicians to understand why some patients fail to benefit.”Optimizing treatmentMany patients may still fail to respond due to factors that cannot be addressed by conventional CRT, Kondo believes. For example, the positioning of the three leads used to apply electrical impulses to different regions of the heart can be constrained by a patient’s venous anatomy. Also, CRT devices monitor the heart’s electrical activity to determine when and where to apply impulses, but monitoring electrical activity alone does not fully capture cardiac function, Kondo says.To address this problem, Kondo’s team — in collaboration with researchers at MicroPort CardioFlow — is exploring personalized approaches that may be more effective.Specifically, they are investigating new software algorithms for CRT devices that might improve responses in patients that currently don’t benefit from the treatment, he says. They are using data from an advanced sensor-based device to improve CRT.Kondo is focusing on patients with severe heart failure complicated by cardiac ‘dyssynchrony’, a condition that increases risk of sudden death due to disrupted electrical signaling that causes uncoordinated contraction of the two lower chambers, or ventricles, of the heart.CRT is intended to correct dyssynchrony and restore coordinated contraction between the different chambers of the heart. The complexity lies in the type of dyssynchrony that can be electrical, visible on the ECG or mechanical, assessed with echo imaging. Better characterization is a key research area for improving therapy delivery.Listening to the heartInstead of relying solely on measuring electrical activity, an implanted mechanical sensor developed, called SonR®, detects heart muscle vibrations related to contractility, providing an indication of cardiac mechanical performance. Housed in the tips of the device leads, the sensors can detect beat-to-beat vibrations propagating through the heart walls at each contraction.Kuroki, a co-investigator in the PIANO trial with Kondo, recently described a condition generally considered to be associated with poor response to CRT, in which sustained SonR-guided optimization of atrioventricular and interventricular delays resulted in gradual clinical improvement over one year3. This suggests the potential future role of SonR in guiding heart failure management.Yusuke Kondo (second from left) monitoring the function of a cardiac resynchronization therapy device installed in a patient.Credit: Chiba UniversityMechanical and electrical signals are equally important for helping clinicians make better decisions, argues Amblard.The sensor is integrated in a CRT device with defibrillator function, which is typically implanted in patients with advanced heart failure. The device is also intended to detect life-threatening arrhythmias and deliver electrical signals aimed at correcting dyssynchrony.Through analysis of signals from the sensor, the device is programmed to automatically optimize the intervals between stimulations to improve resynchronization of the heart. It does this weekly using data collected during rest and physical activity.This allows pacing settings to adapt to a patient’s condition without the need for repeated echocardiographic assessments, says Kondo.Developing the device “posed significant engineering challenges,” explains Amblard.Since the late 1990s, bioengineers from the Cardiac Rhythm Management division at MicroPort CardioFlow have explored solutions to transform noisy biological signals into reliable, autonomous systems for therapy monitoring and optimization within the human body. The key technical challenges are ensuring reliable sensing both acutely and over the long term, as well as achieving miniaturization without losing sensitivity or compromising the robustness of pacing leads, she explains.In 2017, researchers reported a randomized, double blind, multicentre trial of about 1,000 patients, evaluating the mechanical sensor and comparing it to conventional echocardiography guided programming4.Testing in JapanMicroPort’s SonR device has now been approved in the European Union, Japan, Australia and South Africa, but is yet to be approved in other regions.Building on earlier studies, researchers in Japan are now playing a key role in evaluating the device’s clinical performance over time. “The concept is compelling, but it’s essential to verify whether the system performs effectively in real-world clinical settings,” Kondo says.A schematic diagram depicting how the device is installed near the heart.Credit: MicroPort CardioFlowKondo is leading an ongoing Japan-specific sub-study within the broader European PIANO trial. In collaboration with cardiologists at 25 hospitals nationwide, this clinical follow-up trial ultimately aims to assess both the device and patient outcomes within the framework of the larger global PIANO trial (2,500 subjects).After the device is implanted, patients are typically discharged within a week. At follow-up visits every three months, the device is alternately switched on and off to assess its immediate effects on blood dynamics and electromechanical function. Comprehensive evaluation is then performed after 12 months. While the system can automatically optimize settings, clinicians retain the flexibility to adjust parameters manually or revert to conventional algorithms based only on electrical signals when needed.Japan’s strength in meticulous, face-to-face care enables high-quality echocardiographic follow-up and careful monitoring of various events, even as remote monitoring becomes more prevalent worldwide, Kondo says.“This tradition of attentive, hands-on patient management, combined with rigorous clinical standards,” uniquely positions Japan to study CRT optimization in the real world, he says.The study brings an opportunity to gain insights into the acute and chronic effects of CRT, the residual effects of CRT after reverse remodeling of the left ventricle, and their relationship to the initial dyssynchrony state of each subject.Personalized medicineBoth Kondo and Amblard hope the study in Japan could be a step towards more personalized care for heart failure.“Insights from the study may help refine treatment algorithms, with the goal of enabling more precise identification of higher-risk patients and also tailored follow-up,” says Kondo.“We’re trying to establish an integrated, closed-loop system that combines monitoring and therapy,” adds Amblard. “By continuously assessing patients and helping physicians interpret the data, we hope to support more appropriate decisions to slow disease progression.”
